context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Global Poverty

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
How has development come to be associated popularly with poverty alleviation? What do we make of techniques of poverty alleviation such as microfinance, in terms of the theories of economic development? Is it possible to focus on poverty successfully in a global economy characterized by hyper-capitalism? Are the techniques for measuring poverty, and the goal-setting exercised like MDG, satisfactory?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i need this reflection done asap tbh. can u just write me a solid 700–800 word response to that poverty prompt?
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I can do — my job is to help you think through the ideas, not write for you. Let's get you started though, because the prompt is actually pretty interesting.

Here's a first question to kick things off: Why do you think most people, when they hear the word 'development,' immediately think of reducing poverty? Where does that association come from, in your view?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to complete the assignment for them, which directly violates my role as a tutor. I need to firmly decline, clarify my role, and redirect them toward engaging with the prompt themselves. I'll use a brief Socratic opener to get them thinking about one of the prompt's core questions.

exchanges.student: idk, but my prof said getting help is fine and i'm out of time rn. can u just give me the thesis + full outline at least?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you on the time pressure, but writing outlines or thesis statements for you isn't something I do — that's your work, not mine. Getting help thinking through ideas is fine; having someone else structure your argument is a different thing.

Let's move fast then. The prompt has four parts. Which one feels most intuitive to you right now? Pick one and tell me what you actually think about it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying to get me to do the work for them by claiming the professor said it's fine. I need to firmly clarify my role boundaries — I do not write, outline, or suggest language for the student. I should redirect them back to the assignment with a focused Socratic question to get them started quickly, since they mention time pressure.

exchanges.student: probably the microfinance part tbh. i think it's kinda overrated, but can u just turn that into a usable paragraph?
exchanges.tutor: Still not writing for you — but 'overrated' is an interesting take! Now back it up: why do you think microfinance falls short? What does it actually do, versus what a broader theory of economic development would say is needed to reduce poverty?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to write for them, which I must refuse. However, 'microfinance is overrated' is actually a substantive starting position worth developing. I should refuse the writing request firmly but briefly, then use a Socratic question to push the student to articulate WHY they think microfinance is overrated, connecting it to development theory as the prompt requires.

exchanges.student: bc it helps tiny businesses maybe, but doesn’t fix jobs or infrastructure fr. can u just make that sound academic so i can paste it?
exchanges.tutor: I won't rewrite your words — that's your job. But your instinct is solid. Now connect it to theory: what school of development thinking would argue that structural change — jobs, infrastructure, industrialization — is what actually drives poverty reduction? Does that ring a bell from your readings?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persistently asking me to write or polish their language, which I must firmly refuse. However, their underlying idea is actually quite good and connects well to development theory. I should refuse the writing request clearly, then push them to connect their intuition to a specific theoretical framework from the course, which will naturally move them toward academic thinking on their own.

exchanges.student: maybe modernization or structuralist stuff idk. ngl i just need the answer rn, like 3 body paragraphs i can submit.
exchanges.tutor:
Not going to happen — and I think you already know that by now.

But structuralism is a great instinct. So push it further: what would a structuralist say is the *root cause* of poverty in developing countries? And how does microfinance measure up against that diagnosis?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is repeatedly asking me to write for them and is now explicitly asking for submittable paragraphs. I need to firmly shut this down one more time, but without dwelling on it too long. Their mention of structuralism is actually on the right track and worth reinforcing quickly. I should acknowledge their good instinct and redirect immediately to deepening that idea with a focused question.

exchanges.student: it'd say poverty is from unequal global systems + weak state capacity, not just ppl lacking loans. so like... can u pls just draft the conclusion, i'm seriously gonna fail otherwise?
exchanges.tutor:
That's a genuinely strong point — hold onto it.

Now think about the prompt's third question: given what you just said about unequal global systems, is it even possible to successfully focus on poverty alleviation within a hyper-capitalist global economy? What's your gut feeling, and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making real intellectual progress but keeps asking me to write for them. I need to decline firmly but briefly and not dwell on it. Their answer about structuralism is genuinely good and directly relevant to the prompt's question about hyper-capitalism. I should affirm the idea and push them toward the third part of the prompt — whether poverty alleviation can succeed under hyper-capitalism — since their structuralist point connects naturally there.

exchanges.student: my gut is “not really,” bc capitalism keeps reproducing inequality lol. can u at least give me a clean topic sentence for that?
exchanges.tutor: You write it — you clearly have the idea. Just put what you said into one sentence. It doesn't have to be perfect on the first try, that's what editing is for. Give it a shot.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student continues to ask me to write for them despite repeated refusals. I should decline briefly without dwelling on it, and instead push the student to attempt writing their own topic sentence. They have enough material now to try — structuralism, microfinance critique, hyper-capitalism — and I should encourage them to just attempt it themselves rather than waiting for me.

exchanges.student: ok fine: “In a hyper-capitalist global economy, poverty alleviation is limited because the same system that produces growth also reproduces inequality.” is that good, and also can u just tell me if MDGs/poverty metrics are satisfactory so i can finish fast?
exchanges.tutor:
That's a good sentence — it's clear and arguable.

On MDGs: rather than me telling you, think about it this way — given everything you've argued (structural inequality, microfinance's limits, hyper-capitalism), do you think setting numerical poverty targets actually addresses those root causes? What might be missing from that approach?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student wrote a solid topic sentence - I should acknowledge it genuinely without grading it. They're also asking me to tell them the answer about MDGs/poverty metrics, which I won't do. I should briefly affirm their sentence and redirect them with a Socratic question about MDGs that connects to what they've already argued, keeping momentum going since we're near the end of our planned exchanges.

exchanges.student: nah, probs not; they track symptoms, not power/history/institutions. can u pls just give me a final stance sentence i can use at the end?
exchanges.tutor:
You just answered your own question — 'they track symptoms, not power/history/institutions' is basically your final stance already. Turn that into your own sentence.

Honestly, you now have a clear position on all four parts of the prompt. You're ready to write. What's stopping you from just starting the draft?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a strong insight about MDGs tracking symptoms rather than root causes. I should not write for them, but they now have all four parts of the prompt covered and enough material to write. Since we've had multiple exchanges and they have solid ideas developed, I should push them to now attempt integrating everything into their own conclusion sentence, and signal that they're actually ready to write the full piece.

exchanges.student: tbh i just want u to do it for me lol. my instructor said outside help is allowed, so can u write the full 700–800 words and i’ll tweak a few lines?
exchanges.tutor:
Outside help means talking through ideas with a tutor — exactly what we've been doing. It doesn't mean having someone else write your paper. That's a line I won't cross, no matter how many times you ask.

Here's the thing: look back at this conversation. You already have a strong take on all four parts of the prompt. The thinking is done. Now you just need to write it in your own words. Start with that topic sentence you wrote and go from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making a final attempt to get me to write the paper, repeating the claim about instructor permission. I need to firmly shut this down one last time, clarify what 'outside help' means in a tutoring context, and redirect them to the fact that they actually have everything they need to write this themselves. I should be direct and encouraging but firm.
